title: HAVE_LICHEN_LINK vs CONFIG_LICHEN_LINK: two names for the same concept
description:
lichen_l2.c uses two different names for the same condition:

Line 50-57:
```c
#if defined(CONFIG_LICHEN_LINK)
#include <lichen/link.h>
...
#define HAVE_LICHEN_LINK 1
#else
#define HAVE_LICHEN_LINK 0
#endif
```

Then all subsequent guards use HAVE_LICHEN_LINK (lines 71, 183, 302, 372, 428, 591, 615, 632, 668, 734).

The indirection exists because CONFIG_LICHEN_LINK comes from Kconfig and is either defined or not, while HAVE_LICHEN_LINK is always defined (to 0 or 1), enabling '#if HAVE_LICHEN_LINK' syntax that reads more naturally.

What goes wrong: Two names for one concept increases cognitive load. A reader seeing HAVE_LICHEN_LINK must trace back to understand its source.

Rewrite: Use 'IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_LICHEN_LINK)' from <zephyr/sys/util.h> which provides the same 0/1 semantics as HAVE_LICHEN_LINK but uses the canonical Kconfig name. The code would become:
```c
#if IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_LICHEN_LINK)
```
